title: Dynamic Card Game

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-05-stochastic-processes.md

setup.narrative: A standard 52-card deck (26 red, 26 black) is shuffled. The dealer reveals cards one at a time without replacement. Red = +$1; black = -$1. You can STOP at any point and cash out your net.
rules: If you stop, you keep your current net.
Net at any point = (red drawn) - (black drawn).
You can also wait until the deck is empty (then your net is 0 -- forced to stop).
setup.question: What is the expected value of the optimal strategy?

learningObjectives: Set up the value function f(b, r) where b, r = remaining black, red cards.
Recognize the symmetry: net = b - r (current black-red gap reflects the running tally).
Use backward induction with boundaries f(0, r) = 0 and f(b, 0) = b.

stages.label: Set up the value function
stages.prompt: Let f(b, r) = optimal value when b black and r red cards remain. The current net = (initial 26 - r) - (initial 26 - b) = b - r. The DP is: f(b, r) = max(stop = b - r, continue). What's the continuation value?

options.text: Continuation = (b/(b+r)) * f(b-1, r) + (r/(b+r)) * f(b, r-1).  (Conditional probability: next card is black or red.)

stages.explanation: Probability next card is black: b/(b+r). After drawing it, state becomes (b-1, r). Similarly red: r/(b+r), state (b, r-1). Optimal value of the next state is f of that state.

stages.label: Boundary conditions
stages.prompt: What are f(0, r) and f(b, 0)?

options.text: f(0, r) = 0 (no blacks left, every future card is red, so the running net only DECREASES; optimal is stop now at current net = 0 - r ... wait.) Actually f(0, r) = max(0 - r, ...). Since all remaining are red and net only decreases, stopping gives net = -r. Wait, the net at state (0, r) is 0 - r = -r... hmm let me re-check the convention. The convention here: net = b - r where b, r are REMAINING. Then at state (0, r), net is 0 - r = -r, but this is meaningless. The actual current SCORE is (cards already drawn that are red) - (cards already drawn that are black) = (26 - r) - (26 - b) = b - r. At (0, r): score = -r. So f(0, r) = max(-r, all future cards red = same -r). Hmm. The given solution says f(0, r) = 0 -- which means we interpret f as the EXPECTED WINNINGS GOING FORWARD, not the cumulative score. So f(0, r) = 0 since no profit from drawing only reds.

stages.explanation: The convention in the source: f represents the FORWARD-LOOKING value (additional gains you can lock in). At (0, r) all remaining cards are red, each adds -1 to net, so optimal is STOP NOW (no continuation gain). f(0, r) = 0 (no future profit). At (b, 0): all remaining black, each adds +1, so taking them all gives +b. f(b, 0) = b.

stages.label: Symmetric DP recursion

options.text: Yes, but for forward-looking: f(b, r) = max(0, expected forward profit from continuing).  Continuing gains +1 if next is black (and then forward value from (b-1, r)), -1 if red.  Always have the option to stop and lock in 0 going forward.

stages.explanation: Forward-looking: f(b, r) = max(0, (b/(b+r))*(f(b-1, r) + 1) + (r/(b+r))*(f(b, r-1) - 1)). Stop iff continuation value <= 0.

stages.label: Commit
stages.prompt: Working backward from boundaries via DP, the value f(26, 26) = ?
stages.explanation: The optimal stopping strategy adds about $2.62 of expected value over just letting the game run to the end (which would give 0). Despite the SYMMETRIC starting position, the optionality to stop creates positive value -- analogous to American option pricing.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is the value POSITIVE despite the symmetric setup? What 'option value' is being captured?
How does the optimal stopping strategy compare to the strategy 'stop the moment you're ahead'?
Recognize the structural identity with American option pricing -- early exercise = stop early.
